data.title: IREN: Bright Future Ahead - Low-Cost Bitcoin Mining And AI Tailwinds
data.description:
IREN Limited (NASDAQ:IREN) has emerged as one of the best-performing Bitcoin miners this year. Stock is up an impressive 210% since April lows. The company is gradually transitioning from a pure Bitcoin miner into a fast-growing AI infrastructure disruptor. IREN is leveraging its low-cost Bitcoin mining operations to fund AI data center infrastructure. As a result, IREN has avoided issuing new equity or using other debt-fueled instruments to support the company's growth.

Cheap renewable energy has enabled IREN to expand its dual-revenue business model successfully. It's exceptional Q1 2025 results highlight a 280% year-over-year revenue increase ($148.1 million to $54.3 million). Given its recent rally, some investors might assume the stock has already priced in the company's future success, putting extra pressure on management to deliver. However, I see it differently — I believe that, based on fundamentals, the market still values IREN as a traditional Bitcoin miner, with only a modest premium for its AI-focused expansion.

The company is operating at exceptionally high margins, with top-line growth accelerating and bottom-line performance nearly tripling year-over-year ($8.6 million to $24.2 million). It's a profitable, self-funded business that is scaling at a rapid pace — and I tend to believe, the market is still mispricing it.

Despite the recent surge in stock price, I view IREN as an undervalued name with significant upside ahead. At a $3.98 billion valuation, it is not the cheapest crypto miner play. However, I believe it remains largely overlooked due to its legacy business. The full potential of high-yield AI compute revenue has yet to be fully priced in. In my opinion, IREN could outperform the broader market. I rate it as a Buy, with a $20 price target over the next 12 months. AI-focused names are being rewarded by the market, and the current disconnect in valuation presents a clear opportunity.

Monumental Shift: Low-Cost Bitcoin Mining Fuels IREN's Ambitions To Become A Big AI-Infrastructure Name

At the end of June, IREN management announced it had successfully reached the company's mid-year target of 50 EH/s in its Bitcoin mining operations. Management also stated it has no plans to reinvest further in the mining segment. Instead, approximately $500 million in annual profits from Bitcoin mining will be redirected towards AI infrastructure and cloud investments.

Co-Founder and Co-CEO of IREN, Daniel Roberts, has commented:

Reaching 50 EH/s is a defining milestone and a testament to our ability to rapidly deliver complex energy and data center infrastructure. In just 30 months, we’ve scaled organically ~50x to become one of the world’s largest and most efficient Bitcoin miners. With 50 EH/s of mining expansion complete, we’re now turning to our next frontier, leveraging the same execution discipline to scale AI infrastructure across high-growth compute markets.

I believe this marks a monumental shift by management. Also, I don't think it's fully priced into the stock, despite the recent upside. The chart below shows IREN's impressive 67% gain, while most peers have struggled to outperform the benchmark.

This visual clearly shows that since the announcement of a shift toward AI, the market has begun to separate Iris Energy from its pure Bitcoin mining and HODL peers. The stock has significantly outperformed the group. By scaling beyond BTC mining, IREN has reduced its dependence on Bitcoin's extreme volatility and other risks tied to this cryptocurrency. I think this differentiation is important; a dual-revenue business model carries fewer risks and offers greater growth potential.

Profiting From Bitcoin Mining: How Cheaply Can Iris Energy Mine?

Low-cost Bitcoin mining activity is a key driver behind IREN's ability to self-fund its AI cloud expansion and maintain shareholder-friendly capital management. In a recent update, the company disclosed its take-home rate from mining operations:

$41k all-in cash cost per Bitcoin mined reflects all direct and indirect costs for the quarter ended March 31, 2025, including total net electricity costs and other costs (employee benefits expense, professional fees, site expenses, RECs and other operating expenses excluding one-off items), divided by the number of Bitcoin mined during the same period.

Bitcoin price at the end of the first quarter was around $82,000; therefore taking into account all-in cash cost of $41,000, IREN effectively achieved a 100% return on investment for each coin mined. These strong margins should help sustain continued funding for its AI expansion.

The key takeaway is that IREN faces minimal downside risk in its mining segment, with a low likelihood of turning unprofitable. For example, with BTC currently trading around $114,000, even assuming a conservative 3% increase in mining costs to $42,230 per coin — the company would still earn approximately $71,500 in pure profit for each Bitcoin mined.

According to its monthly report disclosed July 7th, IREN mined 620 BTC in June - a 1% decline from the previous month. The slight drop was likely due to a pause in mining activity expansion and increased network difficulty. Based on that, and assuming BTC trades around an average of $114k, we can estimate the potential profitability for the next 3 quarters:

FQ4, 2025 FQ1, 2026 FQ2, 2026
BTC mined: 614 x 3 = 1.842k BTC mined: 608 x 3 = 1.824k BTC mined: 602 x 3 = 1.806k
$71k x 614 = $132 million $71k x 608 = $129 million $71k x 602 = $126 million

Our calculations align closely with management-provided estimates, which project profit of around $500 million annually — most of which will be mainly reinvested in fast-growing and high-potential AI cloud business.

Even if BTC price remains flat over the next 5 months, IREN's mining operations would continue to generate strong profits. In fact, Bitcoin would need to crash by roughly 60% for the company to approach breakeven — clearly indicating limited downside risk. As a result of that, IREN's stock price may not track BTC's volatile trends that much; therefore, it might become more attractive to investors who favor stability.

98% Profit Margin AI Cloud Business: What Really Sets IREN Apart

IREN currently generates only a small portion of its revenue from AI-related activities, but the disclosed 98% profit margin makes this a highly attractive area to expand and scale. On July 3rd, the company announced the acquisition of 2.4k Nvidia Blackwell GPUs, unlocking next-generation AI training and inference at scale.

Since 2023, Iris Energy has been a compelling growth story, driven primarily by its highly efficient and profitable mining operations. Now, the narrative is changing toward becoming an AI-driven dual-revenue name, with the stock price tracking this growth trend, reflecting the clear correlation between the two.

The income statement shows that IREN has doubled its revenue every year since 2023, while the cost of revenue increased by only a small fraction, which is an impressive pace of growth.

According to the company's disclosed monthly update, AI-Infrastructure related revenue remains relatively modest—$2.2 million a month of $67.7 million total defined to 3% of total revenue. However, with the acquisition of the next-gen GPUs, the halt in mining operations expansion, and a focus on scaling software-based AI computing, the situation is set to shift dramatically.

In FQ3 2025, the company provided more details on AI business segments, aligning with the projected growth in AI-related revenue:

  • AI Cloud Services

IREN has begun supplying white-labelled data compute services to leading US artificial intelligence cloud providers. Management expects an annual run-rate of approximately $28 million. They have observed the strong demand for air-cooled Blackwell and steady consumer growth, viewing the projected top-line contribution as sustainable.

  • AI Data Centers

The company plans to launch Horizon 1 — 50 MW liquid-cooled AI data center — in Q4 2025. The expected capital expenditure is $6 to $7 million per MW. Although potential top-line contributions were not disclosed yet, management sees a strong demand and commercial rationale.

Estimating the top-line impact from AI data centers is quite difficult given the Horizon 1 launch later this year. That said, the broader AI data center market is expected to grow from $17 billion today to a staggering $93 billion by 2032, presenting a 26% CAGR. In my view, a pivotal change to an AI-infrastructure-focused business should be rewarded sooner or later.

IREN recently closed an upsized $550 million convertible notes offering — and it's stated that part of the proceeds will be used for ''General corporate purposes and working capital''. While management hasn't provided a full breakdown of how the capital will be allocated, I think it could accelerate AI growth initiatives.

Valuation - IREN Stock Is Compelling: Here's Why

At first glance, IREN may appear expensive, trading at the forward P/E ratio of 118.2 with an expected Forward EPS of $0.14. But evaluating Iris Energy at face value doesn't tell the whole story. The company is growing its top line at a rapid pace while reinvesting most of its profits into AI-infrastructure, which limits near-term bottom-line, but lays the foundation for future growth.

As we have reviewed, the company is profitable, with both revenue segments operating at high margins. However, this isn't clearly reflected in its FCF, which stands at ($35.77 million) on a trailing twelve-month (TTM) basis — largely due to the impact of capital-intensive scale investments. The EV to sales ratios on both: TTM and FRWD basis — respectively 10.90 and 8.07 — may suggest overpricing compared to sector medians of 3.29 and 2.99, but it is typical when investors are willing to pay a premium for strong growth and high margins, which is precisely the case here with IREN.

Iris Energy Sector Median
EV to EBITDA (TTM) 31.82 17.28
EV to EBITDA (FWD) 15.82 14.57

On an EV/EBITDA basis, IREN's valuation is much closer to sector norms. That tempers the idea that the stock is significantly overvalued. I believe that investors recognize that IREN is building a competitive moat in AI data centers, while still being early in the fast-growing landscape. With a structural shift reducing dependency on Bitcoin's price volatility, shareholders may be pricing in a lower risk profile and rewarding the strategic decision with richer valuations.

IREN's balance sheet is strong, with high liquidity and low debt. Net debt stands at $139.7 million — an amount that could be covered by just three months of profits from high-margin BTC mining. Debt-to-equity ratio is 0.22, which is healthy. It suggests an extremely solid capital structure and plenty of room to borrow in case needed for liquidity (though that's currently not the case).

Total debt Shareholder Equity Debt to Equity
$324.1 million $1.425,6 million $324.1 / $1,425.6 = 0.22

On a PEG basis, the stock appears to be pricey, despite its growth projections. Although IREN doubled its revenue over the past two years, I only project 30% top-line growth for the next 12 months. It is a conservative approach because of uncertainties related to the AI data centers. Therefore, applying the respective growth projection, with the forward P/E of 118.20 and forward EPS of 0.14, we end up with a 3.94 PEG ratio.

Projected Growth Forward P/E PEG Ratio
30% 118.2 118.2 / 30 = 3.94

While a PEG ratio above 1 typically signals overvaluation, I am taking a cautious approach by lowering the growth rate, despite the company aggressively reinvesting net income to scale its business. Higher growth projections would make the stock more appealing from a PEG perspective, but conservatism provides a margin of safety against unexpected errors.

I think it is realistic to expect IREN stock to surge to $20 price target in the next 12 months. From a fundamental perspective, the bottom line of the company should reach $0.80 while traded at a 25x forward multiple. This multiple is considered quite low for fast-growing small-cap stocks. That is because businesses, that are operating in the software and information technology landscape, are usually rewarded with such forward multiples while growing at low double-digit rates, rather than doubling their revenue year over year. I think a 40x multiple would be more appropriate — but again, let's leave ourselves a margin of safety.

It would be easier to achieve this EPS target if CapEx — currently ($1,311.4) million at TTM basis, an increase of 3x since FY2024 of ($479,9) - would be reduced. However, Iris Energy is clearly prioritizing top-line growth, with hopes that bottom-line will follow in the future.

Ultimately, it is a growth story. Valuation gives a mixed impression, while debt-related metrics make a stock compelling choice - a high forward P/E, PEG ratio make me wonder if we are overpaying for the stock. Keep in mind that all the calculations above exclude enormous investments planned for AI data centers, which are expected to be launched later this year. The company has managed to grow top line more than 100% for two consecutive years. However, we need to remember, that AI revenue currently accounts for less than 3% of total revenue.

Bitcoin mining's high-margin business enables successful self-fund expansion into the promising AI data center market, which is expected to grow 4x in the next 7 years. IREN has never spent so much in capital expenditures — fairly, it's 3x larger expenditure than the previous year's and 10x more than the year 2023. This shows how confident IREN management is — and I believe it's quite reasonable to pay a larger premium for its future business.

I think that the price target of $20 is reasonable within the next 12 months. Although the forward P/E and PEG ratios outbalance the bullish thesis, Iris Energy is growing fast and AI-focused business projections are justifying trading at a modest premium. Debt-related metrics reveal strong financial management, indicating low leverage. The company is well-positioned for long-term growth and I expect the market to recognize this accordingly.

What Are The Main Near-Term Risks & Concerns? What Are The Areas To Monitor?

Despite positive sentiment surrounding the current stock price performance, the company is exposed to multiple concerns and risks, that could significantly affect its future price action.

As IREN is shifting its business model toward an AI-focused approach, Bitcoin price volatility could significantly affect its stock performance and projected cash flow. Although the all-in cost to mine is low, any sharp drop in BTC price could create challenges in maintaining rapid revenue growth and scaling operations.

At first glance, the 98% take-home margin of its AI cloud business is impressive, but the expected run rate is only $28 million. It's a relatively small amount compared to the infrastructure scale. In case management would have to postpone its launch target of Horizon 1 to a further date, unfulfilled expectations would worsen sentiment, while dragging down the stock price.

Given its the legacy business, it will take some time for the stock market to differentiate IREN stock from its bitcoin mining peers. Despite the recent valuation gap caused by AI announcements, Iris Energy still trades similarly to other small-cap mining stocks. Accordingly, this may limit the potential upside and investors might be reluctant to pay a premium for its business.

I think that management execution is the key. If they deliver on their guidance and prior communications, and BTC experiences further upside in the coming months, I expect that my estimate of $20 price target could be conservative.

Conclusion: IREN Stock Is Not Cheap, But The Market Has Yet To Price Its Full Potential

Iris Energy is a low-cost Bitcoin miner and AI-focused business that is primarily self-funding its transformation to AI data centers and cloud services. Given the stock's price performance, we can see that shareholders welcomed this decision to pause expansion of the mining business and replace it with the fast-growing, high-demand AI infrastructure.

From a valuation perspective, the company is not cheap. It has a robust balance sheet and low debt. The Company has grown its top line at a triple-digit rate for two consecutive years. From both P/E and PEG perspectives, it appears expensive, but the monumental shift to AI is promising. It seems that the market is willing to pay a premium for this potential AI upside. While its legacy business is expected to support the transition to the fairly new landscape, BTC volatility trends could significantly affect its transformation progress.

I think that IREN is a Buy with the stock price target of $20 over the next 12-month period. I don't expect it to massively outperform the broader market, as the transition to an AI-infrastructure business may take time until it starts generating any significant income. The company is on its right path — it will take time, but the company's financials reveal groundwork for success is in place.

12 analysts suggest a one-year average price of $22, implying a 19.83% growth for the stock. As a conservative investor, I would like to leave a margin of safety, which gives me more room to breathe. On a fundamental basis, the company has performed significantly well over the past few years, but long-term investors have only been recently rewarded with a surge of more than 200% since April lows. I estimate it's just the beginning for the company and while temporary upside could be limited, the long-term outlook is bright.

data.title: The Eternal Debate Over Valuations
data.description:
The US stock market’s performance since the dark days of April has truly been something to behold. On April 8, the S&P 500 had fallen by 18.9 percent from its previous record high, set on February 19.

On April 9, of course, the Trump administration punted its “Liberation Day” tariff plans three months down the road, and the markets took off. By the time that three-month pause came due, on August 1, the blue-chip index had risen almost 28 percent from the April 8 low. Good times, if they can be maintained.

What About the Denominator?

But with that stellar price performance comes the usual scrutiny about valuations. This time, the scrutiny is particularly warranted, because valuations by some measures are probing fresh new nosebleed territory. Consider one measure, price to sales (P/S).

We tend to like sales as a denominator for valuation ratios versus the more widely used price to earnings (P/E), for the simple reason that there is less room for distortion and accounting chicanery at the top line of the income statement versus the bottom line.

So let’s look at the forward (next twelve months) price to sales ratio for the S&P 500, going back a quarter century, all the way back to the peak of dotcom mania at the end of 1999.

On average, investors on August 1 were willing to pay $3.08 on average for every dollar of a company’s revenue, slightly more than the $3.06 they were happy to fork over in early 2000, when the stock market crested ahead of its long, three-year slide during which the S&P 500 lost 49 percent from peak to trough.

Now, before anyone asks, we’re not here to say that history is about to repeat itself. Markets, and history generally, don’t work that way. Still, that is a milestone worth thinking about. The forward P/E ratio, which is what we are looking at here, represents the consensus forecasts of securities research analysts who cover these companies.

The forward outlook today is better than it was three months ago; based on the current consensus among analysts covered by FactSet, a market research and analytics company, the outlook for second quarter sales is almost a full percentage point higher than it was four months ago.

But those estimates can always change – and as we noted in our commentary last week, the economic picture for the remainder of the year continues to present more questions than answers.

Then there is the Shiller CAPE – the Cyclically Adjusted P/E ratio developed by Yale economist Robert Shiller that presents average real earnings over a ten-year period to smooth out the fluctuations and vagaries of shorter-term figures.

The current CAPE ratio of 37.8 is not as high as it was in 2000, when it neared 45. But it is still higher than at any other time since 1881, including the three major earlier secular bull markets that peaked in 1901, 1929 and 1966 respectively.

The Staying Power of Irrational Exuberance

As the time-tested saying goes, the market can stay irrational longer than you can stay solvent. Neither the CAPE nor, for that matter, any other valuation metric has ever been a reliable gauge for timing when to get out of the market and when to double down. The same is true today. The fact that prices are by some measures (e.g., sales) higher than they were at the peak of dot-com mania does not mean that they are due for a crash tomorrow.

In fact, the main driver of US stock price performance for the past couple months is largely the same one that has been driving price performance since the beginning of 2023: the AI narrative. The top two companies in the S&P 500 by market capitalization, Nvidia (NVDA) and Microsoft (MSFT), make up fifteen percent of the total market cap for the index.

The top eight companies, which include the aforementioned two plus Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), Broadcom (AVGO), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Tesla (TSLA), account for 34 percent of total market cap, and all of them in one way or another represent a facet of the AI story.

Debates about the practical role of artificial intelligence in the economy versus the theories and the hype abound, but so far the story has weathered a considerable amount of scrutiny without experiencing anything close to a knockout punch.

What investors can do, and what we have been sharing as strategic thinking with our clients this year, is to diversify exposure to US large-cap equities away from levels that may have been prudent two years ago but are (in our opinion) less certain today.

Non-US asset classes, both equities and fixed income, deserve closer consideration. So do certain nontraditional asset classes. Trying to time the inflection points is a fool’s errand. Patient and careful diversification, we believe, is a better approach for the long term.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Looking For Balance Between Risk And Return: ECF And Its Preferred Stock
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield

In this article, we will look for a good balance between risk and return in the closed-end funds, which have a high credit rating and offer a stable income. In the following lines, we will provide you with more details about Ellsworth Growth and Income Fund (NYSE:ECF) and its preferred stock (NYSE:ECF.PR.A).

Fund Overview

The fund was founded in 1986, ECF has around $187mln in total assets at the end of the second quarter of 2025 and 14mln outstanding shares, spread across approximately 92 holdings. The biggest investments in the portfolio are Broadcom, Akamai Technologies Inc., and Bloom Energy Corp. Moody's rates the fund with an "A1" credit rating.

At the current moment, the market price of ECF is $10.54, with a NAV of $11.93 and a negative 1-year Z-score of 0.02 and a positive 3-year Z-score of 0.55. It's trading at an 11.65% discount. It has a leverage of 16%, and the only source of it is the outstanding preferred shares. The expense ratio is 1.44% and the non-leverage expense ratio is close to 1.26%:

The dividend yield is around 6.07%, and the annualized distribution rate on NAV is 5.36%. ECF is paying its shareholders a $0.52 yearly dividend in the form of $0.1300 quarterly distributions:

Below you can see the 10-year historical premium/discount chart of the fund:

And here is a 3-year total return comparison between ECF and its peers:

Preferred stock

ECF has one preferred stock:

(ECF.PR.A) trades below par, at a market price of $21.44. Its current yield is 6.12%, and its current stripped yield is 6.17%. Its call date is passed, and it is callable anytime. ECF issued 1.2 million shares of ECF.PR.A on 09/13/2017. The stock pays cumulative distributions of 5.25% per annum, or $1.31, in the form of $0.328125 per quarter.

The preferred stock doesn't have a credit rating from the major credit agencies. As we said above, ECF has a credit rating of "A1", so the credit rating of ECF.PR.A would be 2 notches down, or an "A3" equivalent.

Conclusion

ECF is a closed-end fund with a good reputation, supported by a high credit rating of "A1" from Moody's. ECF.PR.A has a current yield of about 6.17% and is trading below par. Although the preferred shares themselves do not have an official rating, it can be assumed that they have an approximate "A3" rating, making them suitable for more conservative investors seeking a balance between income and risk.

data.title: Subaru: Caution Is Warranted; Focusing On 2026-2027E (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Subaru (OTCPK:FUJHY) as a business is one of the attractive fundamentals, at least theoretically. I've made money on this company twice over a period of 24 months, buying and selling in what I view as a very good execution of valuation-oriented investment logic. In my last article, the company significantly outperformed the market. In this article, compared to the last article, the same thing is not true. While I was at a "BUY" on Subaru in the latest piece, I also cautioned, as clearly as in the title, about purchasing company shares at that valuation given the outperformance. Subaru underperformed the market since that article, though by less than 5-6% in total.

Find my last article on the company here - in this one, we'll look at Subaru's latest results about 6 months after my last article. Japanese stocks can be tricky investments - but I've now extended my coverage, from about 2 stocks 2 years ago, to almost 8 at this point, and expanding it going forward as well. Subaru remains a core one of these covered holdings.

Subaru is not a holding for me at this time. From this perspective, my last article was perhaps not 100% "on point" - because following a few weeks, I sold off my last share when it climbed well above the $9/share mark for the ADR. At this time, I don't have shares - but I may purchase some going forward if I can get a good valuation.

Let's look at the company's positives and negatives.

Subaru is viewed neutrally by the market, and I see some of the challenges as well.

One of the first things I look at in a company is the value of the products they sell. And Subaru does not disappoint here. The cars, which are the company's namesake products, are known for a variety of positive qualities. A high resale value, strong reliability, and a strong demand in the used car market are only a small part of the appeal here. I have personally seen the resale reliability and appeal, as in environments which are known for "rougher" roads and contexts, such as the African outback, are known for only a few brands for appeal - Toyota (TM) and Subaru primarily among them, with Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY) another example here.

In terms of an investment, Subaru exhibits, at least in periods, a lower volatility than some of its automotive peers (and higher than others), but unfortunately also a lower yield, and a high yield is usually one of the arguments for investing in an automotive like this one. Subaru, even after the decline and a dividend bump, only has a 4% yield, while many of the other automotives are at close to double the size/level we have currently. The company released its full-year 2025A results about a month ago, during late June, with the full release back in mid-May.

The company's production was down 2.4%, with unit sales down about 4% - not the greatest of trends. Even worse was a significant operating decline in the double digits, despite good sales trends (due to the introduction of new models, good FX, and the like). The company's operating profit nonetheless declined due to higher sales incentives.

What's worse, the company has given us estimates for the next fiscal, 2026, and the forecast here is for further decline, for about 950,000 going down to about 900,000 in total for the year. The company is, in fact, "aiming" for this level, which of course opens up a potential for a lower result as well. Operating profit as of the company's own forecast is yet to be determined. The main reason for this is the continued uncertainty from tariffs and geopolitical developments. It, among other things, has the company's forecast at a close to 70% operating profit/EPS decline for the 2026 period, coming to no higher than ¥145/share, which will barely cover the dividend forecast at ¥115/share for the year. That dividend is mostly confirmed at this time.

The challenges faced by Subaru are not limited to tariffs and geopolitical however. It's in the midst of a CapEx phase - building its Yajima plant in preparation for the BEV production the company is targeting, which is set to produce the aforementioned 900k units. Even the exact level of CapEx is uncertain at this particular point.

It's this uncertainty that's pervasive in the market now, and it's not just limited to Japan. Even as I am writing this article, the US markets are falling after poor employment numbers and revised expectations, with some suggesting a "stall" in the economy. If these things are what we're seeing in the US, we shouldn't be surprised that similar trends are in the countries supplying cars to the US either.

It's this uncertainty and volatility that I want to convey in this article. Subaru's fundamental qualities are solid - even if we see a year or two of declining earnings and results, unless the company somehow changes its way of doing business, reversal and/or earnings stability is likely to come. We can look at forecasts for the coming few years, and what they tend to have in common is a slump going into 2026, but a slight reversal and stability going forward, all the way until 2027-2030E.

EBITDA and earnings-related KPIs, meanwhile, are likely to crater to their lowest levels in well over 10 years in 2026E. This assumption will form the nucleus of my updated thesis for Subaru, and will be the reason that I am lowering my PT - so take a look at what you see here, and the associated recovery for the company as well.

Meanwhile, investors will not be left out in the cold during this decline. The company has already stated that its dividend of ¥115/share will be maintained even in the next year, and while it will not grow massively over the coming few years, the implied stability of this shareholder return is nothing to sneeze at - especially in Japan, where companies typically have lower dividends than we see in areas like Europe or NA.

Using the current earnings estimates and reversal potential, Subaru does not look like a good investment at a ~ ¥2,800 share price, which is where the company currently trades. We'll see in valuation, but it means that most of the current automotive sector picks have a better yield, a better rating (credit), and a better overall upside due to a better production base. Subaru, while building impressively resilient vehicles, has a small production base, and the company's production volume of building is less than a fifth of a bigger company, such as BMW (OTC:BMWKY).

We've therefore reached a point where I believe the company does not represent an attractive "BUY" - and this is despite the company going to a price level where I could previously have considered it a "BUY".

I will be lowering my assumptions for Subaru for the short-term, and using a short-term and longer-term thesis to make it clear where I believe the company to be investable here.

Subaru Valuation - no attractive upside due to the prospective 2026E decline, I see a better upside in other automotives

It's not as though I was unclear on the risk for Subaru in my last article, but I will bring some more things to the table here. In my last piece, I worked with a ¥2,950 PT for the native ticker. This is going down to ¥2,250 PT, which is a significant cut.

Following the estimated decline in 2026, I don't believe that due to a mix of continued CapEx, increased tariffs, competition, and macro (as well as lower profit from BEV vehicles), Subaru to be able to rise back to over ¥400 in EPS all that easily. For the coming years, I don't believe the company will go above ¥300/share. Using that estimate for 2028E, I believe the company's value should be around the 20-year average with a ¥300 EPS estimate, which comes to roughly ¥3,200/share - that's the fair value.

The PT for this takes into account that the company is likely to be volatile, so I'd want a 15% annualized upside, not just to 11-12, but to 10.5x as well. As it stands, the upside for Subaru, even at something like an 11.6x forward P/E, is very limited.

The current company price targets from other analysts range from a ¥1,850 low to a ¥3,300 high, with an average of around ¥2,500, down from almost ¥3,600 average about a year ago at the same time. Out of 14 analysts, only 2 are now at a "BUY", with a majority of 9 at a "HOLD" rating for the reasons mentioned above.

While it's possible to see market-beating upside in both a long perspective, and based on a higher premiumization, I believe the right choice to make at this time is to wait - either for the company to drop significantly lower as a result of what's going on here, or what's coming in the next year or more - then invest once it drops low enough.

For automotive alternatives, I'd look at BMW, which, even at a 9x P/E, has an upside of over 21% per year even here. BMW is also A-rated, and a yield that's higher than Subaru's - over 5% at this time for the native BMW ticker.

The same is true for Mercedes (OTCPK:MBGAF), with ticker MBG - only the yield for that company is over 8%. So there's a significant potential for attractive alternatives here.

Combining the forecasts and peer appeal here, I believe it's quite clear that Subaru is simply no longer investable for the time, and both the Quant, Analyst, and Wall Street ratings are correct in being "Hold", not higher (and not lower). If in the negative now, I would not sell; however, I would also not buy it here.

Risks to my updated thesis are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The risks to my neutral thesis are outperformance on the part of Subaru, and the qualities that make the company so attractive shining through, such as the reliability and the quality of the vehicles overall. However, I very much doubt, especially in light of technological changes, dependent on the US and Japanese market, and the economic and tariff uncertainty, that this will be enough to drive alpha for Subaru when considering where earnings are likely to go in the next year.

So for these reasons, I don't consider it likely that the company will outperform here in the next 1-2 years, at least not at this price.

My updated ¥2,250 PT comes to an ADR PT of $7.6/share.

Thesis

  • Subaru is a USA-exposed automotive company with a small aerospace arm. It has fundamentally appealing products, perhaps suffering a bit from being behind some of its competitors, and having almost no market share in Europe. But overall, they have great products, and are still a good business as of 2025.
  • Due to the addition of tariffs and geopolitical uncertainty, the earnings decline has increased significantly until 2026, and now justifies a significant change in price target.
  • I significantly lower my PT for the company. I now believe it is worth ¥2,250, and given that the company is closer to 20% above this price target, I now rate Subaru a "HOLD". There are significantly more compelling automotive investments to make.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of ¥2,250/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of August 2025.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Buy Technology?
data.description:
Our quick answer from a quantitative perspective is no. Of the 15 Sectors covered in reports by ValuEngine, the Technology sector ranks fourth in expected 12-month price return according to our forecast model. Our sector ratings are equally weighted, indicating that this broad sector has strongly rated stocks throughout the market cap spectrum and beyond the “Magnificent Seven.” In fact, 570 of the roughly 4,000 US-listed stocks covered by ValuEngine are in the Technology Sector. Of these, roughly 32% or 180 stocks are currently rated 4 (Buy) or 5 (Strong buy). However, 110 of these stocks have less than $20 million in market capitalization and would fail many institutional investors’ liquidity and/or fiduciary screens. Thus, we narrowed this week’s analysis to the 70 stocks that pass this screen.

While all 70 of these stocks are forecast to post significantly higher price returns than the median stocks we rate according to our predictive model, they are not necessarily favored by ValuEngine’s valuation model. In fact, 63 of the 70 are rated as overvalued. This means that while the forecast model predicts further price appreciation, the stocks are already overvalued and therefore more risky. If we remove these as too risky, this leaves 7 Technology Stocks with more than $20 million in market cap that are currently selling below what we calculate as Fair Value. All of those happen to be rated 5 (Strong Buy) as well.

Here is the list of the tech stocks that passed all of these screens:

All seven stocks of the above companies are rated 5 (Strong Buy) and are priced below what we calculate as “Fair Value.” We determine Fair Value by multiplying the current price by (1 +/- the percentage by which the stock is undervalued over overvalued according to the ValuEngine Fair Value model). According to this formula, ZM is nearly 100% undervalued. The undervaluation levels calculated for IFFNY, XYZ, TWLO, and HUBS are also quite large. MRVL and NOW are just a bit undervalued and could be thought of as constituting opportunities for growth at a reasonable price, sometimes referred to by the acronym GARP.

As detailed in the above descriptions, only the German semiconductor company Infineon is not headquartered in the USA. As far as “The Magnificent Seven” are concerned, most of them are rated 4 or 5 as well. Nvidia (NVDA) and Meta Platforms (META) are the highest rated at 5. Turning to Technology ETFs, we look at the nomenclature system and data from ETFdb.com, a TMX VettaFi company, to set the table for us. There are 175 ETFs classified as Technology. Of those, 41 are leveraged and/or inverse and excluded from this analysis. Of the 134 that remain, 89 are indexed and 45 are active.

This table highlights the top 20 tech ETFs in descending order by Assets Under Management.

Analysis:

  1. The largest, most-traded and best-known ETF in this group is QQQ with the formal name Invesco QQQ Trust Series I. The trust uses modified market-cap weighting to hold the stocks in the Nasdaq-100 index. The latter is an index of the 100 largest non-financial and non-utility companies listed on the Nasdaq exchange. As such, it is not a pure technology index. Non-tech holdings include Costco Wholesale (COST), PepsiCo (PEP) and Amgen (AMGN). It also does not include technology stocks such as IBM with primary listings on the NYSE. However, it is close enough that it has become the go-to Technology ETF for most investors and the vast majority of traders. That said, and as we’ve repeated a number of times in the past: When buying new shares of QQQ, first consider QQQM, the Nasdaq-100 ETF. It holds exactly the same portfolio at a fee of 0.15% which is 5 basis points lower than the 0.20% expense ratio of QQQ, saving investors 0.05% in annual returns. In either form, QQQ or QQQM, the ETFs are both rated 4 (Buy) by ValuEngine.
  2. The next two in line are VGT, the Vanguard Technology ETF and XLK, the Technology Select Sector SPDR from State Street Global Advisors. Both are pure plays in the US technology sector, and both charge the same economical expense ratio of 0.09%. Like all the Select Sector SPDR ETFs, XLK applies modified market cap weighting to all the stocks in a given sector of the S&P 500, in this case 70 stocks. Most other sectors are not as broad. That said, investors looking for broader exposure may feel they are getting more for the same expense ratio with VGT which holds 321 US technology stocks and includes midcap and some small-cap stocks in addition to the large cap stocks that dominate the ETF’s weighting. Fifth-ranked IYW, iShares US Technology Index ETF has only 25% of the assets of VGT despite starting five years earlier and for good reason. IYW offers less-broad exposure but charges about four times the expense ratio. The final broad technology US Sector fund actually has the lowest expense ratio, 0.08%. It is FTEC, the Fidelity MSCI Information technology ETF and holds a very similar portfolio to VGT. Thus far, the one-basis point differential has not been enough to entice most institutional holders of VGT to switch to FTEC. At any rate, the best returns in this category year-to-date and annualized over 5 years has gone to XLK, the narrowest ETF in this group. All four of these US-Tech-industry-exposure ETFs are rated 3 (Hold) by ValuEngine.
  3. All the other ETFs in this grouping are either more targeted, use different weighting schemes, or hold some or all non-US stocks. The top performer year-to-date in this group is ARKK, the ARK Innovation ETF with better than 35%. Although no longer the US AUM leader among all active ETFs, ARKK certainly is the top AUM actively managed ETF within the Technology Sector. However, on a 5-year annualized basis, ARKK has the second lowest annualized return, -0.6%, in the group. After a magnificent beginning performance-wise which served to legitimize actively managed ETFs as an alternative to the popular indexed ETFs and to the less efficiently structured traditional mutual funds, ARKK’s returns began to suffer late last decade. The fund now has a feast-or-famine reputation in terms of potential to realize superior returns, which is fair given that markets are cyclical. Historically, the top outperforming actively managed funds tend to flip when market cycles change. ARKK’s AUM of $7.2 billion may be significantly down from its all-time peak but certainly is #1 among the 45 actively managed funds serving this sector.
  4. Next on the list of year-to-date price gainers is KWEB, KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF. It is the only non-US single-country-focused ETF in the top twenty, and demand for new shares has been rising this year in tandem with its price. It is #10 overall on the chart in Assets Under Management. However, there is also considerable currency exposure. In the five-year period, its annualized return is -9%, almost entirely because the renminbi had declined versus the dollar through most of this period before rebounding sharply this year.
  5. The final ETF with a return above 20% is SMH, the VanEck Semiconductor ETF. It is also a very impressive fourth in AUM. With a close to 30% annualized five-year return, it is the leader in that category by a wide margin. Although most of this can be attributed to the fact that semiconductors have outperformed most other technology industries and sub-sectors, SMH has significantly outperformed and garnered more assets than the other semiconductor ETF, SOXX from iShares.
  6. Other subsectors and specific industries within tech covered by ETFs in the group include: software, internet commerce, cybersecurity, artificial intelligence, cloud computing and pioneering technologies. Two are globally rather than US-focused. Finally, two of these ETFs, RSPT and QTEC, are variations or subsets of QQQ.
  7. Although several Technology subsector ETFs are rated 5 (Strong Buy) from ValuEngine, only one is in the top 20 in Assets Under Management. That ETF is FDN, the First Trust Dow Jones Internet Index Fund. Smaller ETFs, as ranked by AUM, that are currently ranked 5 (Strong Buy) include: BUYZ, Franklin Disruptive Commerce ETF; NBDS, Neuberger Berman Disrupters ETF and SEMI, Columbia Select Technology ETF.
  8. Only two of the ETFs offer dividend yields higher than 1%, KWEB and TDIV. This is expected since the technology sector is known as a growth sector, providing relatively few value or income opportunities.

In summary, while many strategists are advising people that the high valuations in technology stocks and ETFs augur that it is time to dump this group and rotate into value stocks, our forecast model disagrees, seeing the above-average growth supported sufficiently by above-average earnings-related streams and strong contributory factors. On the other hand, a preponderance of overvalued stocks according to our valuation model also means that above-average upside opportunities are accompanied by significantly above-average risks.

data.title: PG&E Corporation: 3 Catalysts In The Next Month Supporting A Buy
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Option Alert as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Why Is PG&E Promising?

PG&E Corporation (NYSE:PCG) experienced a significant hit on its stock price since the end of 2024 due to the wildfire across California. However, recent bullish option flow may suggest something opposite.

In this article, I will provide a fundamental overview of PG&E, a blended multiple/DCF model for value estimation, and investigate three possible catalysts in the next month that could lift its stock price. I estimate fair value at $19.10 a share, about 28% upside from the recent $15.00 quote.

Company Overview

PG&E, founded in 1905, is the largest electric and gas utility company in the state of California, serving more than 16 million people in northern and central California across 70,000 square mile. However, since 2018, the stock price has experienced several hits due to wildfire liability, as shown in Figure 1. The most recent wildfire at the end of 2024 cause a nearly 40% drop on the stock price from $21 to $13. Even though PG&E was not liable for the fire, it shared the same wildfire fund pool with Southern California Edison power company, who was liable and depleted the shared fund. As a result, the wildfire risk was no longer cushioned by enough wildfire fund. Although a proposed management plan is to invest$6~7 billion to upgrade the grid network through 2028, which can flow into PG&E rate-base earnings (California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) sets rates that target a 10.25% allowed return on equity), traders still seem to worry about the future risk of the company.

Figure 1. PG&E stock price

(Source: Yahoo Finance, by 8/8/2025)

The Options Chain

As shown in Table 2, the option chain of $13~20 expiring on 09/19/2025 exhibits a very low put/call ratio of 0.05, with nearly 700,000 out-of-the-money calls open at the $16 strike (374k contracts), $17 strike (155k contracts), and $18 strike (201k contracts). Puts at $15 and $17 (~20k and 21k contracts, respectively) were opened primarily last year during the wildfire time for hedging purposes. These numbers suggest that a special event could occur for PG&E.

Valuation

Market-multiple cross-check

To estimate Enterprise Value/ Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization (EBITDA) of PG&E, we list here some large-cap peers: Southern Co. (SO), Duke Energy (DUK), Edison International (EIX), and Sempra Energy (SRE), which have roughly 10x forward EBITDA. Thus, we apply 10x to PG&E's 2025E EBITDA ($9.2 billion) and the equity value is equal to 10 x $9.2 billion - net debt = $39.4 billion

Given 2.17 billion shares outstanding, the estimated fair stock price is equal to equity value / outstanding shares = $18.2

Table 3. PG&E and peers EV/EBITDA

(Source: Yahoo Finance, by 8/8/2025)

Five-year DCF

Base-year Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF): $4.5 billion (mid-point guidance)

Growth: 5% on cap-ex-driven earnings lift.

FCFF(5-year) = FCFF(base) x (1+5%)^5 = 5.74 billion

Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC): 7.5% (10 Year Treasury Rate 4.2%, beta 0.7, Equity-risk premium 5.5%, pre-tax cost of debt 6%, Statutory tax rate: 27.98%, 52/48 capital mix)

Terminal growth: 2% perpetual

Terminal Value (TV) = FCFF(5-year) / (WACC-2%) = $106.5 billion

Present value of terminal (PV_terminal) = TV / (1 + WACC)^5 = $74.2 billion

Present value of explicit period (PV_explicit)= $20 billion

Estimated value = PV_terminal + PV_explicit = $94.2 billion

Share price = (Estimated value - net debt)/outstanding shares = $18.9

Value-based Price Target

Value-based Price Target = 0.6 x DCF share price + 0.4 x cross-check share price = $18.6

Three Catalysts in the Next Month

On the basis of the above blended multiple/DCF valuation, there are three upcoming catalysts that could potentially lift the stock price of PG&E to as high as $23.6 by September 19 if all of them land.

Re-capitalizing $18 billion Wildfire Fund

On 07/30/2025, Governor Gavin Newsom proposed to draft legislation to re-fill 2019 California Wildfire Fund, which is almost depleted now, with another $18 billion. The money will be collected from both the utility companies and the ratepayers. PG&E is estimated to pay around $4 billion over the next 20 years, which is equivalent to a cheap but comprehensive wildfire insurance of only $200 million per year. The draft is estimated to be introduced on August 18 when the legislature reconvenes from Summer Recess.

Back in July 2019, when the original $21 billion California Wildfire Fund was approved, PG&E stock price jumped about 16% in two days. If the proposal can be passed to the floor by Fiscal committee by August 29 and receive the floor vote on September 12, the stock price would rapidly reach its value-based price target $18.6 with a possible 16% surge.

Twenty-year License Extension for Diablo Canyon

In 11/2023, PG&E applied for a 20-year operational extension for its Diablo Canyon nuclear station, the only nuclear power station in the state of California. A timeline of this proposal is shown in Table 4. And, after receiving the final supplemental environmental study from the Nuclear Regulatory Commission on 06/27/2025, it is currently pending the final vote due in late August.

Table 4. Current License Renewal Review Schedule

For context, Vistra Corp. (VST) stock price jumped roughly 10% when its renewal for its Perry facility in Ohio was approved in July. Vistra had a market cap of about $58 billion at that time. Considering PG&E's fundamentals, an approval of the 20-year extension is likely to result in a 15% rally, also bringing the stock price to its value-based price target. The renewal is likely to be approved given political support from both the state level, proven by earlier approval of 5-year extension from CPUC in December 2023, and the white-house level, proven by the recent Trump Administration's proposal to build nuclear reactors on the moon.

CPUC Ruling on Diablo Canyon Cost Recovery

PG&E requested to get reimbursed by $761 million for operating Diablo Canyon nuclear power station, and 95% of the ask, $722 million, was approved on 07/25/2025, according to a tentative decision from CPUC. The decision is now open to public comment / replies until mid-August, and a Commission vote can happen as early as August 28 on the CPUC business meeting. Historically, an increase in stock price of around 3~5% can be expected within a week after a clean approval of major cost-recovery filings (e.g., the 2014 General Rate Case Proceedings, 2021 wildfire-bond authorization, and 2023 General Rate Case Proceedings).

Table 5. PG&E Catalysts Date Window

Table 6. Catalyst Effects

One out of three hits 40% EV/EBITDA 10x $18.2
No wins / terrible fire season 20% EV/EBITDA 7.5x $13.7
Catalyst-based Price Target $19.5

Blended Price Target

Based on the valuation and catalysts described, I created a blended price target as follows:

Blended Price Target = 0.5 x Value-based Price Target + 0.5 x Catalyst-based Price Target = $19.1

Risks

Wildfire Liability

Wildfire liability is still the most significant risk for PG&E, despite the proposed refill of the Wildfire Fund with $18 billion. For example, if a multi-billion-liability fire happened again (hopefully not), the Wildfire Fund can quickly be used up, and the future risk of wildfire can no longer be cushioned. This could tank the stock price back to $12~13 range, assuming $0.80 per share for every $1 billion of incremental pre-tax liability.

Legislative Delay

Delay in legislation can suppress the recent momentum. For example, if the $18 billion wildfire-fund bill failed to pass by the September recess, the wildfire risk could not be fully cushioned, the stock price could experience a 5% drop due to disappointment. Besides, an unexpected request, although very unlikely given the current political favor of nuclear power, for additional environmental data could delay the NRC vote into 2026 and cause another 5% drop.

Interest-Rate Shock

Although very unlikely given recent rate-cut requests from the Trump Administration, a 100 basis-point rise in the 10-year Treasury would lift PG&E's implied cost of equity and trim about $0.70 from DCF value.

Trade set-up

Equity play: Accumulate shares at $15.00 or below with a 2-month price target of $19.1 (~28% upside)

Options angle: Buy the Sep $16 call for about $0.6. If the shares touch $19.1 before Sep 19, the payoff is roughly 5:1 on capital at risk.

Conclusion

PG&E, sitting at $15.00 now, has a fair value of $19.1, based on market-multiple cross-check, DCF model, and three potential catalysts ($18 billion Wildfire fund, Diablo Canyon 20-year extension, and CPUC ruling) coming in the next month. The stock price could be pushed to as high as $23.6 if all three catalysts land. Although some risks are still lingering (Wildfire liability, Legislation delay, Interest-rate shock), the probability of the three risks is significantly cushioned by both the bullish option flow and the current political & economic environment.

data.title: Increasing Earnings Vol
data.description:
The average stock that has reported earnings this week (more than 600 companies) has seen a one-day share price reaction of more than +/-8.5% (absolute change). That's much higher than the 20+ year average absolute move of 5.75% that US-traded stocks have historically experienced on their earnings reaction days. (What this means is that in modern markets, the average stock can expect to see a one-day move of +/-5.75% once per quarter following its earnings release. Yes, a large portion of a stock's move over its lifetime comes from the one trading day each quarter after it reports earnings.)

Notably, stocks have been getting more volatile on their earnings reaction days in recent years. Below is a chart showing the average one-day absolute percentage change that stocks saw in reaction to earnings by year going back to 2001 using our Earnings Explorer tool.

In the early 2000s before the Financial Crisis, investors could expect the average stock to move a little over +/-5% on its quarterly earnings reaction day. The average move on earnings then spiked to record levels above +/-7% during the Financial Crisis, when overall market volatility increased dramatically and investors sold everything like their life depended on it for a couple of years.

From 2010 to 2015, things settled down again and the average stock once again moved about +/-5% on earnings. Since 2015, however, we've seen a general uptrend in earnings day volatility for stocks. In 2018, the average move on earnings ticked back above +/-6% for the first time since the Financial Crisis. Aside from a random dip back down to 5% in 2021, daily vol on earnings has ticked higher and higher back up near 7% in 2024 and 2025. So far this year, the average stock that has reported has seen a one-day move of +/-6.86%.

Another way to chart long-term earnings-day volatility is to look at the rolling 10,000-earnings report average one-day move for US stocks that have reported since 2001. Each point on the blue line in the chart below shows the average one-day change on earnings that the prior 10,000 stocks reporting have experienced.

Interestingly, the spike in earnings vol during the Financial Crisis came during a nasty bear market when equities were drawing down sharply for more than a year. The recent move higher has come during a period when US equities have been in an uptrend for multiple years.

In the current day and age of easy, commission-free trading on brokerage apps available right on your smartphone, share price volatility in reaction to stock-specific earnings news has moved increasingly higher. At the same time, overall market volatility hasn't seen a similar increase, which means that more and more of a stock's overall performance is coming from the one trading day per quarter when it posts its financial results and forward guidance.

Original Post

data.title: The Most Lucrative Spread In Global Markets You've Never Heard Of
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA & Aneeka Gupta

Looking at gold in August 2025, forget spot prices for a moment. We believe what matters most in gold mining isn't the number on the screen, it's the spread between what you're paid and what it costs to keep the machines running. In gold today, that spread is breathtaking. At over $3,300 per ounce and climbing, gold prices have surged to all-time highs.1 But here's the kicker: the median all-in sustaining cost (AISC) across gold miners2 barely scrapes past $1,600.3 That's roughly $1,700 of production margin4 per ounce—the kind of figure that should turn heads in any sector. It's not just a bull market in gold—it's a new reality in gold mining profitability.

Figure 1: The Golden Spread: Mining Margins Surge to Record Highs

The Iron Law of Margins—and Why It's More Important Than Price

Figure 2 makes it explicit: what powers decision-making inside mining companies isn't spot price in isolation—it's the durability and scale of margin.

Figure 2: Cost Contained, Price Unchained: Gold's Production Margin Hits All-Time High

The 2011–2013 cycle burned that lesson into corporate memory. Back then, gold ran to $1,900, but miners chased growth with irrational exuberance—funding marginal projects at the top of the cost curve, and torching investor capital when the cycle turned. Today? Discipline reigns. New projects must clear a 15%–20% internal rate of return at a conservative long-term price deck. Boards are skeptical of spot prices.5 They assume gravity wins eventually.

Why Costs Are Rising—and Why That's Not a Bad Thing

There's nuance in the rising AISC trend. Yes, input inflation and environmental, social and governance (ESG) capital expenditures play a role—solar-battery hybrid power plants don't come cheap, nor does skilled labor in remote jurisdictions. But here's the subtlety: some of the rising cost is actually a byproduct of choice. When margins expand, miners lower their cut-off grades and process more low-grade ore—effectively "printing" more gold.6 This endogenous cost inflation is a feature, not a bug. It's how miners stretch throughput and maximize net present value (NPV), using today's margins to unlock marginal ounces they'd never touched in leaner times.

The Silent Constraint: Why Supply Won't Catch Up Anytime Soon

You'd think these record margins would trigger a flood of new supply. But the supply curve in gold mining is glacially inelastic. Projects take 7–10 years from discovery to first pour. And the pipeline is tapped out—exploration budgets were slashed post-2013, and high-grade deposits are increasingly rare. Layer in tougher permitting regimes, higher royalties, and ESG constraints, and you get a simple truth: there is no fast track to new gold supply, no matter how juicy the economics look today.7 This is a structural bottleneck, not a cyclical hiccup.

Figure 3: Once Burned, Twice Cautious: How Gold Miners Rebuilt Their Capex Playbook

The Setup: Durable Margins, Disciplined Management and a Repricing of Risk

What's unfolding is the kind of margin regime shift that quietly rewrites investor expectations. Many gold miners are now gushing free cash flow with balance sheet leverage under 0.3 times EBITDA8—offering oil-major-style dividend frameworks, but with lower capex intensity and less political heat. That kind of profile deserves a re-rate. Investors don't need a higher gold price to get paid. They just need margins to persist. And here's the asymmetric part of the story: even if gold drops $400, margins remain historically fat.9 That's a setup few sectors can match—and one that feels deeply underappreciated right now.

What about the Case for Gold in Summer 2025?

In summer 2025, the macro case for gold remains as compelling as ever—not because it hasn't moved, but because its move reflects a deeper re-rating of global financial conditions. Crossing $3,300 doesn't mean gold has "run too far"—it means investors are recalibrating what's worth trusting. Real rates remain subdued, and while the U.S. debt burden is drawing more scrutiny, it's less about hitting a breaking point and more about signaling a shift in how markets and policymakers frame long-term risk. Central banks, especially in emerging markets, have been quietly but persistently reshaping their reserve strategies—accumulating gold as a non-sovereign asset that sidesteps the geopolitics of Treasuries.10 Add in simmering global tensions and rising skepticism toward fiat durability, and gold's ascent starts to look less like a spike and more like the early stages of a structural repricing. The price has moved—but the world around it may have moved even more.

Building the Investment Strategy

What is the optimal way to express that exposure? Is it physical, futures-based, miner equities, royalty and streaming models, or something more novel—like tokenized gold or gold-integrated multi-asset strategies? In 2025, gold is back in the portfolio construction conversation. But the debate is shifting from "why gold" to "which gold—and how."

So, let's consider some of the more popular choices:

  • Do they take direct exposure to the metal itself, through vehicles like SPDR Gold Shares (GLD),11 and position for stability and monetary debasement hedges?
  • Or do they reach for equity-like upside via the shares of gold miners in the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX),12 which historically offer leveraged sensitivity to movements in the gold price—but come with operational risk and higher volatility?

Or, there is a third option that has entered the arena—capital-efficient exposures that combine gold and gold miners into a single vehicle, such as the WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Gold Miners Strategy Fund (GDMN).13 These aim to deliver both the stability of the metal and the growth potential of the miners, while preserving portfolio capital to be deployed elsewhere.

Figure 4: Standardized Performance

  • 2025 has been a breakout year for gold miners—but not all miners are equal. GDMN has surged 76% year-to-date, handily outperforming both traditional miners (GDX +55%) and bullion itself (GLD +26%). This isn't just beta—it's margin-aware, disciplined alpha.
  • Outperformance is persistent, not episodic. Over the one-year and three-year horizons, GDMN has outstripped its peers by 20–35 percentage points. Importantly, it's done so while the S&P 500 has lagged dramatically in 2025, underscoring GDMN's role as a differentiated return source—not merely a defensive hedge.
  • Longer-run results show structural leadership. Even over the longest common period, GDMN leads with a 27.9% total return, versus 19.3% for GDX and low double digits for GLD and the S&P 500. This suggests an evolving regime where quality-focused miners, strong margin capture and capital discipline are beginning to get re-rated—perhaps durably.

Figure 5:Miners Outshine Gold—and Nearly Everything Else

Gold sets the tone, but miners amplify the story—on both sides. When GLD posts a positive year, GDX historically surges higher, averaging 24.8% returns versus GLD's 16.9%. But the reverse is just as true: in GLD-negative years, GDX has averaged a punishing −12.9%, making it a high-volatility expression of gold sentiment.

  • 2025 YTD is the pattern on steroids. With GLD up 26.3%, GDX has gained 55.4%—but GDMN, the quality-focused miner Fund, has exploded higher by 76.0%. This isn't just leverage to the metal—it's margin expansion and capital discipline working in tandem.
  • Direction matters more than magnitude. The calendar-year table shows that the binary—whether gold is up or down—is a better predictor of miner performance than how much gold moves. GDX tends to trade on trend conviction, not price precision.

Figure 6a:Cycles within the Cycle: How Gold's Direction Has Shaped Miner Returns

Figure 6b:Gold Has Set the Direction—Miners Have Set the Pace

Conclusion: The Margin Regime Has Changed—So Should the Allocation

Gold is no longer just a metal; it's a margin machine. And gold miners are no longer just a leveraged play—they are increasingly capital-disciplined operators that have learned from past cycles. The structural spread between gold prices and AISC has created a margin profile few sectors can replicate, with free cash flow surging and balance sheets healthier than ever. This isn't about chasing price—it's about capturing durable economic rent in a capital-light, cash-rich framework that's finally being recognized by the market.

Against that backdrop, investors are being presented with a timely and underappreciated opportunity: to rethink how gold exposure fits into the broader portfolio. Traditional bullion positions may hedge macro instability, but they don't harness the upside of expanding margins. Miners, selectively deployed, can. And strategies that integrate both exposures while maintaining capital efficiency—blending defensive ballast with cyclical torque—are now proving their worth in real-time performance. In this margin-driven regime, the allocation conversation shifts from "Do I own gold?" to "Am I capturing the full stack of what gold can deliver?"

Figure 7: Additional Information
